title: DMM Bitcoin Custody Hack — 4,502 BTC Stolen
estimatedLossNote: Approximately 4,502.9 BTC stolen, valued at approximately USD 305 million based on BTC market price at the time of the incident. Figure confirmed by DMM Bitcoin company disclosure. Individual customer loss figures are subject to the account transfer process to SBI VC Trade completed in early 2025.
summary: On 31 May 2024, Japanese cryptocurrency exchange DMM Bitcoin suffered an unauthorised transfer of 4,502.9 BTC, valued at approximately USD 305 million, from the company's Bitcoin wallet. DMM Bitcoin restricted buy orders and withdrawals following the breach. The attack was subsequently attributed to the North Korean state-sponsored Lazarus Group (TraderTraitor threat cluster) by a joint advisory of the US Federal Bureau of Investigation and Japan's National Police Agency in December 2024. DMM Bitcoin announced the closure of its exchange in December 2024 and transferred all customer accounts to SBI VC Trade by early 2025.
narrative:
DMM Bitcoin Co., Ltd. was a cryptocurrency exchange operated by DMM Group, a Japanese internet conglomerate. The exchange held BTC and other digital assets in custody on behalf of retail and institutional customers.

On 31 May 2024, DMM Bitcoin detected an unauthorised outgoing transfer of 4,502.9 BTC from the exchange's Bitcoin wallet. The company immediately restricted buy order services and temporarily halted external cryptocurrency withdrawals. DMM Bitcoin confirmed the theft via official channels and stated that customer JPY-denominated deposits remained protected under the exchange's asset segregation requirements under Japan's Payment Services Act. The company subsequently stated that it planned to procure equivalent BTC through a group company to compensate affected customers in BTC terms.

Blockchain forensics firms tracked the stolen BTC as it was moved through a series of layering transactions, a pattern consistent with prior Lazarus Group money-laundering tradecraft. On 23 December 2024, the FBI and Japan's National Police Agency jointly issued a public advisory attributing the attack to the TraderTraitor threat cluster — a North Korean state-sponsored actor previously attributed responsibility for multiple large-scale cryptocurrency thefts including the Ronin Bridge exploit of 2022.

DMM Bitcoin announced on 2 December 2024 that it would cease exchange operations and transfer all customer accounts and assets to SBI VC Trade Co., Ltd. The transfer was completed in early 2025. The company's group backstop commitment meant that customers did not suffer permanent losses from the theft itself, though the exchange closure imposed costs and disruption on customers. The specific technical vector of the private-key compromise was not publicly confirmed at the review date.

timeline.event: Unauthorised transfer of 4,502.9 BTC detected. DMM Bitcoin restricts buy orders and external withdrawals.
DMM Bitcoin confirms theft via official notice. States plans to procure equivalent BTC through group company to compensate customers.
DMM Bitcoin announces closure of exchange operations. Customer accounts to be transferred to SBI VC Trade.
FBI and Japan National Police Agency issue joint advisory attributing attack to North Korean TraderTraitor threat cluster.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Supervisory oversight — exchange security review and customer account transfer process
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Exchange continued operating under FSA supervision through orderly wind-down. Customer account transfer to SBI VC Trade completed. No separate public enforcement action announced.

regulatoryResponse.regulator: US Federal Bureau of Investigation / Japan National Police Agency
regulatoryResponse.actionType: Joint attribution advisory — North Korean state-sponsored threat actor (TraderTraitor / Lazarus Group)
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Public advisory issued. Ongoing law enforcement investigation into movement of stolen assets.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: the theft arose from a failure in the custody of exchange-held Bitcoin — a private key compromise or supply-chain attack on signing infrastructure. In all plausible scenarios, the failure implicates the integrity of the asset custody arrangements. Settlement Finality: the irreversibility of on-chain Bitcoin transactions meant that once the unauthorised transfer was confirmed on-chain, recovery was only possible through law enforcement action or off-chain remediation — the settlement finalised adversarially and unconditionally.

lessons: The DMM Bitcoin hack reinforces that even large, regulated exchanges operating under established legal frameworks remain vulnerable to sophisticated state-sponsored threat actors. The irreversibility of on-chain settlement means that preventative custody controls — including hardware security modules, multi-party computation signing, and offline key ceremony procedures — are the primary defence. Post-incident compensatory mechanisms (asset repurchase commitments, group company backstops) are not a substitute for robust custody security. The FBI/NPA joint attribution also illustrates the growing capacity of law enforcement to identify on-chain actors even when stolen assets are laundered through layering operations.

title: Hong Kong VASP Licence Application Withdrawal Wave — May 2024
primaryEntity: Multiple virtual asset trading platforms (OKX HK, Gate.HK, Bybit, Huobi HK, HKVAEX, and others)
hkRelevance: This entry records the systemic withdrawal of VASP licence applications by multiple major global crypto exchanges from the Hong Kong SFC regulatory regime in May 2024, coinciding with the end of the transitional licensing period under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (AMLO, Cap. 615). The withdrawals marked a significant restructuring of Hong Kong's virtual asset trading landscape and prompted an SFC enforcement campaign against unlicensed platforms.
estimatedLossNote: No direct financial losses attributable to the licence application withdrawals have been reported. Customers of the withdrawing exchanges who had assets on Hong Kong-facing platforms were required to withdraw their funds. The broader impact was a reduction in the number of licensed virtual asset trading venues available to HK investors, potentially increasing concentration risk in the remaining licensed platforms.
summary: Under the mandatory virtual asset trading platform (VATP) licensing regime under Hong Kong's Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (AMLO, Cap. 615), pre-existing unlicensed crypto exchanges were required either to obtain a VASP licence from the SFC or cease Hong Kong operations by 31 May 2024. Multiple major global exchanges — including OKX HK, Gate.HK, Bybit, Huobi HK, and HKVAEX — withdrew their VASP licence applications in May 2024, citing regulatory requirements they were unwilling or unable to meet. Separately, the SFC issued a public warning against MEXC on 15 March 2024 for operating without a licence. By end of 2024, licensed platforms included HashKey Exchange, OSL Digital Securities, and four newly approved exchanges.
narrative:
The Hong Kong SFC introduced a mandatory licensing regime for virtual asset trading platforms under the AMLO (Cap. 615) in June 2023. Platforms already operating in Hong Kong on a 'deemed-to-be-licensed' basis (having submitted licence applications) had until 31 May 2024 to either receive their licence or cease Hong Kong operations. Platforms that had not applied were required to stop immediately.

In May 2024, a significant number of major global exchanges announced withdrawal of their VASP licence applications. Confirmed withdrawals included OKX HK on approximately 24 May 2024, Gate.HK, Bybit, Huobi HK, and HKVAEX. Each cited varying reasons, but the common theme was an inability or unwillingness to meet the SFC's operational, capital, and compliance requirements for licensed VATPs.

The withdrawals followed a March 2024 SFC notice warning about MEXC — a global exchange that had not applied for a Hong Kong licence but was reportedly still accessible to Hong Kong users. On 15 March 2024, the SFC issued a public warning that MEXC was operating in Hong Kong without a licence and added it to the SFC's Suspicious Virtual Asset Trading Platforms Alert List.

The May 2024 withdrawal wave significantly impacted the competitive landscape for virtual asset trading in Hong Kong. Licensed platforms as of end 2024 included HashKey Exchange and OSL Digital Securities (licensed prior to the deadline) and four newly approved exchanges announced in December 2024: HKbitEX, Accumulus, DFX Labs, and EX.IO.

By early 2025, the SFC's Suspicious Virtual Asset Trading Platforms Alert List had grown to over 90 entries, reflecting both the scale of unlicensed activity and the SFC's intensified enforcement posture toward platforms operating without regulatory authorisation.

timeline.event: SFC issues public warning that MEXC is operating in Hong Kong without a VASP licence and adds MEXC to the Suspicious Virtual Asset Trading Platforms Alert List.
OKX HK announces withdrawal of its VASP licence application and cessation of Hong Kong operations.
Gate.HK, Huobi HK, HKVAEX, and Bybit announce withdrawal of VASP licence applications ahead of the 31 May 2024 deadline.
VASP licensing transitional period ends. Unlicensed platforms required to cease Hong Kong operations. Licensed platforms: HashKey Exchange and OSL Digital Securities.
SFC announces four new VASP licences: HKbitEX, Accumulus, DFX Labs, and EX.IO, expanding the licensed platform count to six.

regulatoryResponse.regulator: Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC)
regulatoryResponse.actionType: Public warning — unlicensed VATP operation (MEXC); Alert List additions for multiple suspected unlicensed platforms
regulatoryResponse.outcome: MEXC added to Suspicious Virtual Asset Trading Platforms Alert List. SFC Alert List grew to over 90 entries by early 2025. No formal criminal charges against withdrawing application platforms confirmed.

rarm.explanation: Legal & Jurisdictional: the mass withdrawal illustrates that stringent regulatory requirements can cause large market participants to exit rather than comply, reducing regulated market depth and liquidity — a direct consequence of the SFC's mandatory licensing framework under AMLO Cap. 615. KYC/AML & Permissioning: the SFC requirements that precipitated withdrawals included robust AML/KYC infrastructure, client asset safeguarding, and operational compliance standards. Secondary Market Liquidity: the exit of multiple large global exchanges reduced the number of SFC-licensed trading venues, concentrating HK retail and institutional trading activity in a smaller number of regulated platforms.

lessons: The May 2024 VASP withdrawal wave demonstrates that implementation of a stringent mandatory licensing regime can cause significant restructuring of a jurisdiction's virtual asset market, with large globally-operating exchanges choosing to exit rather than meet local regulatory requirements. For regulators designing VASP licensing frameworks, the episode illustrates the trade-off between rigorous investor protection standards and market breadth. For investors, the episode highlights the operational risk of maintaining assets on platforms with uncertain licensing status.

title: Gala Games — Unauthorised GALA Token Minting from Compromised Admin Wallet
estimatedLossNote: Gala Games reported that approximately 5 billion GALA tokens were minted from a compromised administrative wallet on 20 May 2024. At prevailing market prices, 5 billion GALA represented approximately USD 200–216 million in market value. The company subsequently burned approximately 4.4 billion GALA from its treasury. Permanent losses to token holders are not separately quantified; the incident caused significant but temporary price disruption. Gala Games stated that the funds obtained through the unauthorised mint had been recovered, but this has not been independently verified.
summary: On 20 May 2024, approximately 5 billion GALA tokens were minted from a compromised administrative wallet belonging to the Gala Games ecosystem. The newly minted tokens were sold into secondary markets, causing a sharp price decline. Gala Games responded by working with exchanges to freeze portions of the sold tokens and burning approximately 4.4 billion GALA from the company's treasury to offset the inflationary impact. Gala Games characterised the incident as an internal security breach of an administrative wallet and stated that the situation was resolved and the proceeds recovered.
narrative:
GALA is the primary utility and governance token of the Gala Games platform, a blockchain-based gaming ecosystem. Gala Games controls certain administrative privileges over the GALA token contract, including the ability to mint new tokens using privileged administrative wallet addresses.

On 20 May 2024, an unauthorised party accessed a Gala Games administrative wallet and used it to mint approximately 5 billion GALA tokens. At the market price prevailing at the time of the incident, this quantity had an approximate market value of USD 200–216 million. The minted tokens were then sold into decentralised and centralised exchanges, generating significant selling pressure and causing the GALA spot price to decline sharply — by approximately 15–20% at peak impact, according to market data sources.

Gala Games moved quickly to contain the incident. The company worked with centralised exchanges to identify and freeze portions of the sold tokens. Gala Games also executed a treasury burn of approximately 4.4 billion GALA to offset the inflationary effect of the unauthorised mint on total supply. The company characterised the incident as a compromised internal wallet and stated publicly that the funds had been recovered.

The specific identity of the party responsible, whether internal or external to Gala Games, was not publicly confirmed. The precise technical mechanism of the wallet compromise was not disclosed in Gala Games' public communications. No regulatory agency issued a public response to the incident and no charges were publicly announced.

The incident illustrates the governance risks associated with token contracts that vest centralised administrative minting authority in a small number of wallet addresses without multi-party authorisation requirements.

timeline.event: Approximately 5 billion GALA tokens minted from compromised administrative wallet. Tokens sold into secondary markets; GALA price declines sharply.
Gala Games works with exchanges to freeze portions of sold tokens and burns approximately 4.4 billion GALA from treasury holdings.
Gala Games issues public statement describing incident as a security breach and stating funds had been recovered.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: the incident arose from a failure in the access control governing mint authority over the GALA token contract. Administrative minting capabilities, controlled by a small number of privileged wallet addresses without multi-party signing requirements, represent a concentrated custody risk. Secondary Market Liquidity: the unauthorised tokens were sold into secondary markets, causing significant price dislocation and temporary liquidity disruption for existing GALA holders.

lessons: The Gala Games incident illustrates the governance risks inherent in token contracts that vest centralised minting authority in administrative wallets without multi-party signing, time-locks, or hard supply caps enforced at the smart contract level. For tokenized asset issuers, the ability to mint new supply is a significant governance risk that should be subject to the same rigour as private key custody. The episode also demonstrates that even if an issuer recovers stolen funds through exchange cooperation, the secondary market impact and reputational damage from a supply inflation event are difficult to fully reverse.

title: JPEX — Criminal Charges Filed and Trial Proceedings Commence
hkRelevance: This entry records the progression of criminal proceedings in Hong Kong courts following the mass arrests in September–October 2023. See jpex-vatp-scandal-2023 for the full background to the JPEX case.
estimatedLossNote: Loss figure carried from jpex-vatp-scandal-2023: approximately HKD 1.59 billion (approximately USD 204 million at prevailing exchange rates) in investor complaints received by police as of September 2023. This figure represents reported losses as at the time of the SFC warning and may be revised through the criminal proceedings.
summary: Following the mass arrests and SFC enforcement action relating to the JPEX virtual asset platform in September–October 2023, Hong Kong prosecuting authorities in 2024 filed formal criminal charges against multiple defendants. Defendants include alleged platform principals and social media influencer promoters (KOLs) who marketed JPEX to retail investors. Charges include conspiracy to defraud, operating an unlicensed virtual asset trading platform, and money laundering. Proceedings are at various stages in the Hong Kong courts as of the review date. This is the largest virtual asset fraud prosecution in Hong Kong to date.
narrative:
The background to this entry is the JPEX virtual asset platform scandal that became public in September 2023 when the SFC issued a public warning and Hong Kong Police arrested over 60 individuals, including prominent Cantonese-speaking social media influencers who had actively promoted the platform to retail investors. For the full background, see jpex-vatp-scandal-2023.

In 2024, the Hong Kong Police Force's Commercial Crime Bureau and the Department of Justice brought formal criminal charges against a number of individuals arrested in connection with the JPEX case. The charges brought included, but are not limited to: conspiracy to defraud investors, operating a virtual asset trading platform without a licence, and money laundering offences under the Organised and Serious Crimes Ordinance (OSCO), Cap. 455.

The prosecution of influencer promoters is particularly significant from a regulatory precedent perspective. The case tests whether paid promoters of unlicensed financial platforms — who did not directly operate the platform — can be held criminally liable for their role in soliciting retail investor participation. Such a precedent, if established, would have wide implications for how virtual asset service providers and platforms may use social media marketing in Hong Kong.

Criminal proceedings were heard in the Hong Kong District Court and, in respect of more serious charges, the High Court. Committal proceedings for certain defendants were conducted in the Magistrates' Courts before being committed to the higher courts. As of the review date, some defendants had entered pleas and were awaiting sentencing or trial; others had contested the charges and trials remained pending. No final verdicts or sentences have been publicly confirmed in this database.

Multiple defendants formally charged in Hong Kong courts. Charges include conspiracy to defraud, unlicensed VATP operation, and money laundering. Proceedings ongoing.
Criminal proceedings advance in Hong Kong courts; trial dates being fixed for contested matters. No final verdicts as of review date.

This entry focuses on the legal proceedings dimension: the prosecution tests the scope of criminal liability for platform principals and KOL promoters operating without a licence in Hong Kong, and will establish precedents for the legal and jurisdictional obligations of virtual asset platforms serving HK retail investors.

lessons: The JPEX prosecution is the first Hong Kong criminal prosecution to include social media influencer promoters alongside alleged platform principals in a virtual asset fraud case. The outcome of the influencer charges will establish a precedent for the criminal liability of paid promoters — with significant implications for how virtual asset and tokenized asset platforms may engage KOL marketing in Hong Kong. The case also tests the prosecution of cross-border virtual asset fraud where the platform's principals operated from outside Hong Kong.

title: First Digital Trust / FDUSD Solvency Allegations
hkRelevance: First Digital Limited is a Hong Kong-licensed trust company operating under the Trust Companies Ordinance. It is a Hong Kong-based issuer of FDUSD and has applied for authorisation under Hong Kong's Stablecoins Ordinance. The HKMA monitored the situation.
estimatedLossNote: No confirmed losses. FDUSD maintained its USD 1.00 peg throughout the episode. Justin Sun's allegations of USD 456 million in redemption failures were disputed by First Digital and not substantiated by regulatory findings or court records as of the last review date.
summary: On 2 April 2024, Justin Sun, founder of the Tron blockchain, publicly alleged via social media that First Digital Trust (FDT), a Hong Kong-licensed trust company and issuer of FDUSD stablecoin, was insolvent and unable to honour a USD 456 million withdrawal demand. First Digital Trust denied all allegations, stating that all FDUSD reserves were intact and all client assets were fully recoverable. FDUSD maintained its USD 1.00 peg throughout. The HKMA stated it was monitoring the situation. First Digital Trust subsequently announced legal proceedings against Justin Sun for defamation. No regulatory findings of insolvency or redemption failure were made.
narrative:
First Digital Limited is a Hong Kong trust company licensed under the Trust Companies Ordinance. It launched First Digital USD (FDUSD) in June 2023 as a USD-pegged stablecoin. FDUSD publishes monthly reserve attestations prepared by Prescient Assurance, showing reserves held in US Treasuries and cash equivalents.

On 2 April 2024, Justin Sun, founder of the Tron blockchain and a significant figure in the cryptocurrency industry, published allegations on social media platform X (formerly Twitter) stating that First Digital Trust was insolvent and had failed to honour a USD 456 million withdrawal request. Sun alleged that client assets were impaired and that First Digital was 'practically insolvent.'

First Digital Trust issued an immediate denial, stating that all FDUSD reserves were fully intact, all client assets were recoverable, and that the company was solvent. First Digital noted that any disputes relating to specific clients were separate from the solvency of the FDUSD reserve fund. The HKMA issued a brief statement indicating it was monitoring the situation and would take appropriate action if needed.

FDUSD maintained its USD 1.00 peg on major secondary markets throughout the episode, with only minor temporary fluctuations. Market participants appeared to treat the peg maintenance as evidence against the insolvency allegation. First Digital Trust subsequently announced it had instructed lawyers to pursue defamation proceedings against Justin Sun.

As of the last review date, no regulatory findings of insolvency or reserve impairment have been made by the HKMA, SFC, or any other regulator. No court has adjudicated the underlying factual dispute. The allegations remain disputed. This entry will be updated if regulatory findings or court judgments are issued.

First Digital Trust issues formal denial, stating all FDUSD reserves are intact and all client assets are recoverable.

FDUSD maintains USD 1.00 peg; major exchanges report normal trading. CoinDesk and Bloomberg cover dispute.
First Digital Trust announces legal action against Justin Sun for defamation.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Monitoring statement
regulatoryResponse.outcome: No regulatory action taken. HKMA stated it was monitoring. No findings of insolvency or reserve failure published.

sarm.explanation: Reserve quality: the allegations, if substantiated, would indicate a reserve quality failure. That FDUSD maintained its peg and no regulatory findings of impairment were made suggests reserves were intact. However, the episode illustrates the importance of timely, granular reserve disclosure beyond monthly attestations — the uncertainty created by the allegations was partly attributable to the inability of market participants to independently verify real-time reserve status. Redemption mechanics: the specific allegation was of a large-scale redemption failure. First Digital's denial was not adjudicated. For the SARM analysis of FDUSD as a Stablecoins Ordinance applicant, this episode is noted as a test of redemption credibility under public stress — the peg held, but the underlying facts remain disputed.

lessons: The First Digital dispute illustrates that even where a stablecoin issuer maintains its peg, public allegations of reserve impairment can cause reputational damage and secondary market volatility. The episode underlines the value of continuous (rather than monthly) reserve transparency reporting — real-time proof of reserves would have enabled market participants to independently assess the allegations without waiting for issuer statements. For Hong Kong stablecoin issuers applying under the Stablecoins Ordinance, this incident is a case study in the disclosure dimension of SARM.

title: TrueUSD — Reserve Opacity and Recurring Secondary Market De-peg Episodes
issuerOrOperator: TechTeryx Ltd.; Archblock Inc. (formerly TrustToken, Inc.)
estimatedLossNote: No confirmed aggregate loss figure. TUSD experienced recurring secondary market de-peg episodes during 2024, trading below USD 1.00 for sustained periods. Losses to holders who transacted below peg are not quantified in any public regulatory or court filing.
summary: TrueUSD (TUSD) experienced recurring secondary market de-pegging episodes during 2024, accompanied by sustained concerns about reserve opacity following the transfer of the TUSD brand and management from TrustToken (Archblock) to TechTeryx Ltd. Blockchain analytics firm ChainArgos published research in late 2023 identifying that TUSD reserves were held within a fund administered by a Bahamas-domiciled investment vehicle rather than in the direct bank deposits and short-duration US Treasury instruments described in prior disclosures. Gaps in the reserve attestation schedule further reduced secondary market confidence. The situation illustrates risks associated with stablecoin brand transfers that alter reserve structures without adequate public disclosure.
narrative:
TrueUSD (TUSD) was launched in 2018 by TrustToken, Inc. (subsequently rebranded Archblock Inc.) as a regulated US dollar stablecoin with reserves held in bank accounts, subject to monthly attestation by independent accounting firms. The TUSD brand and operational management was transferred to TechTeryx Ltd., a Cayman Islands-registered entity, during 2022–2023. The precise corporate and beneficial ownership structure of TechTeryx was not disclosed fully in public communications at the time of the transfer.

Following the brand transfer, the reserve structure of TUSD diverged from the model described in prior public disclosures. Blockchain analytics firm ChainArgos published research in December 2023 and January 2024 documenting that TUSD reserves were held not in direct bank deposits or US Treasury instruments, but in units of a fund administered by a Bahamas-domiciled investment vehicle. The credit, legal, and operational risk profile of fund-intermediated reserve holdings differs materially from direct bank or T-bill holdings: fund-held reserves introduce layers of counterparty risk, potentially longer redemption timelines, and reduced transparency regarding underlying assets.

In addition to the reserve structure concerns, the TUSD reserve attestation schedule experienced notable gaps: periods during which attestation reports were not published for more than 30 days, contrary to the monthly disclosure cadence that had been presented as a key transparency feature of the stablecoin.

These developments caused TUSD to trade at a persistent discount to USD 1.00 on major secondary markets during 2024. The de-peg was not acute on any single day but rather reflected sustained, elevated market uncertainty about reserve quality and redemption reliability.

Binance, which had heavily promoted TUSD in mid-2023 as a preferred substitute for BUSD following the SEC's enforcement action against Paxos's BUSD issuance, subsequently reduced its promotional support for TUSD. No regulatory body has publicly announced formal enforcement proceedings against TechTeryx Ltd. or any associated entity as of the review date.

timeline.event: ChainArgos publishes research identifying TUSD reserves held within a Bahamas-domiciled fund rather than direct bank/T-bill holdings.
TUSD attestation schedule gaps become publicly documented. Stablecoin begins sustained secondary market discount below USD 1.00.
TUSD attestation gap exceeds 30 days. Secondary market discount widens. Binance reduces promotional support for TUSD.

sarm.explanation: Reserve Quality: ChainArgos research and attestation gaps indicate TUSD's reserve structure diverged from the direct cash and T-bill backing represented in prior disclosures. Reserves held in a Bahamas-domiciled fund introduce credit, legal, and operational risks absent in direct bank or T-bill holdings. Redemption: the sustained secondary market discount below USD 1.00 — while not a formal redemption failure — indicates reduced market confidence in redemption-at-par. Governance: the brand transfer from TrustToken/Archblock to TechTeryx, without transparent public disclosure of the new reserve custodial chain, constitutes a governance failure. The SARM framework evaluates governance and disclosure together; this incident implicates both.

lessons: The TUSD situation demonstrates that stablecoin brand transfers can constitute material changes to the risk profile of a stablecoin without triggering formal disclosure events or regulatory review. Investors and platforms that rely on historical attestation records for due diligence may not detect reserve structure changes that occur between attestation periods or following a change in controlling entity. The incident reinforces the case for: (i) real-time reserve transparency via on-chain proof-of-reserves; (ii) regulatory requirements for disclosure of material changes to reserve custodians; and (iii) periodic re-evaluation of stablecoin risk assessments rather than reliance on initial or historical due diligence.

title: Hounax Fraudulent Virtual Asset Platform
hkRelevance: Hounax operated directly targeting Hong Kong retail investors, promising high and consistent investment returns. The SFC issued a public warning and Hong Kong Police are investigating.
estimatedLossNote: Approximate USD equivalent of HKD 159 million in reported losses, as cited in SFC warning dated November 2023. Figure reflects reports from affected investors; subject to ongoing police investigation.
summary: Hounax was a virtual asset trading platform that was not licensed by the SFC and targeted Hong Kong retail investors with promises of high and stable returns. The SFC issued a public warning about Hounax on 2 November 2023, noting that the platform was not licensed for virtual asset trading or fund management activities in Hong Kong. Investor losses of approximately HKD 159 million (approximately USD 20.5 million) were reported to police at the time of the SFC warning. Hong Kong Police and the SFC are conducting investigations. The case forms part of a pattern of unlicensed platform fraud cases in Hong Kong during 2023.
narrative:
Hounax operated as an online virtual asset platform claiming to offer investment services in virtual assets. The platform advertised high and consistent returns to retail investors, a characteristic common to many fraudulent investment schemes. Hounax was not listed on the SFC's register of licenced virtual asset trading platforms.

On 2 November 2023, the SFC issued a public warning notice identifying Hounax as a potentially fraudulent platform offering unlicensed virtual asset services to Hong Kong investors. The warning noted that Hounax was not licensed by the SFC for dealing in securities or virtual asset trading, and that the platform's claims of returns were unsubstantiated. The SFC also noted reports of investors losing access to funds when attempting to withdraw.

At the time of the SFC warning, approximately HKD 159 million in investor losses had been reported to the Hong Kong Police Force. Hong Kong Police subsequently commenced an investigation. The SFC referred the matter to police for further action.

The Hounax case occurred in close temporal proximity to the JPEX scandal (September 2023) and contributed to the SFC and HKPF increasing public awareness efforts regarding unlicensed virtual asset platforms. The SFC maintained a public warning list of suspicious virtual asset platforms, and Hounax was added to this list.

As of the last review date, investigations by the Hong Kong Police Force are ongoing. No formal charges have been publicly announced. The identities of the platform operators have not been publicly confirmed in police or court filings.

timeline.event: SFC issues public warning about Hounax. Platform not licensed by SFC for virtual asset or securities services. Reported investor losses approximately HKD 159 million.
Hong Kong Police confirm receipt of multiple complaints and commencement of investigation.

regulatoryResponse.regulator: Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC)
regulatoryResponse.actionType: Public warning — suspected unlicensed virtual asset services and fraud
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Warning issued. Hounax added to SFC warning list. Matter referred to police.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Criminal investigation — suspected fraud
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Investigation ongoing. No charges publicly announced as of review date.

rarm.explanation: Legal & Jurisdictional: Hounax operated without SFC licensing, representing a fundamental legal compliance failure. The case illustrates that unlicensed platforms can target HK retail investors through online channels regardless of the platform's physical location. KYC/AML: high and consistent promised returns are a recognised red flag indicator for investment fraud under FATF guidance and are inconsistent with legitimate virtual asset investment risk profiles.

lessons: The Hounax case forms part of a broader pattern of unlicensed virtual asset platform fraud targeting Hong Kong retail investors in 2023. The consistent offer of high, stable investment returns was the primary marketing mechanism — a pattern common to investment fraud across asset classes. Retail investors in tokenized assets should verify platform licensing status through the SFC's licensed VATP list and treat guaranteed-return claims as a significant risk indicator.

title: JPEX Unlicensed VATP Operation and Alleged Fraud
issuerOrOperator: JPEX (operator entity, corporate structure under investigation)
hkRelevance: JPEX operated primarily targeting Hong Kong retail investors. The SFC issued a formal public warning on 13 September 2023. Hong Kong Police received over 2,627 complaints from investors. Multiple arrests were made in Hong Kong. The case resulted in the SFC accelerating its VATP licensing enforcement activities.
estimatedLossNote: Approximate USD equivalent of HKD 1.6 billion in losses reported by complainants to Hong Kong Police as of late 2023. This figure reflects losses reported by investors and may not represent final confirmed losses. The precise total is subject to ongoing criminal proceedings.
summary: JPEX was a virtual asset trading platform that operated in Hong Kong without holding a valid licence from the Securities and Futures Commission under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance regime for Virtual Asset Service Providers. On 13 September 2023, the SFC issued a public warning about JPEX, citing failure to apply for a VATP licence and suspected market misconduct. Following the warning, JPEX raised withdrawal fees substantially and suspended certain services. Hong Kong Police subsequently received over 2,627 complaints from investors reporting combined losses of approximately HKD 1.6 billion (approximately USD 204 million). Multiple arrests were made including promoters, influencers, and individuals associated with JPEX operations. Criminal proceedings are ongoing.
narrative:
JPEX marketed itself extensively in Hong Kong through social media, KOL (key opinion leader) promotions, and offline events. The platform offered high-yield staking products to retail investors. JPEX had not applied for or obtained a Virtual Asset Service Provider licence from the SFC under the licensing regime for centralised virtual asset trading platforms that took effect on 1 June 2023.

On 13 September 2023, the SFC issued a public warning stating that JPEX had not applied for a VATP licence, that its website made unsubstantiated and potentially misleading claims about regulatory status, and that SFC was investigating suspected regulatory contraventions and market misconduct. The SFC referred the matter to Hong Kong Police.

Within days of the SFC warning, JPEX announced a substantial increase in withdrawal fees from approximately USDT 1 to USDT 999 per withdrawal, which effectively locked out retail investors from accessing their funds. JPEX attributed this to liquidity issues with third-party providers. Hong Kong Police commenced an investigation, and on 18 September 2023, police conducted operations and made initial arrests.

Between September and December 2023, Hong Kong Police arrested over 60 individuals in connection with the JPEX case. Among those arrested were persons alleged to have acted as JPEX promoters, influencers who had promoted the platform to retail investors, and individuals involved in platform operations. The case became the largest virtual asset fraud investigation in Hong Kong's history by number of complaints.

As of the last review date, criminal proceedings involving multiple defendants are ongoing. The SFC has cited the JPEX case as evidence of the need for robust enforcement of the VATP licensing regime and investor education about unlicensed platforms.

JPEX announces increase in withdrawal fees to USDT 999 per transaction. Investors report inability to access funds.

Additional arrests including individuals connected to JPEX promotion activities.
Hong Kong Police report total complaints exceeding 2,000; estimated losses reported by complainants approach HKD 1.5 billion.
Criminal proceedings against multiple defendants ongoing. Total complaint count exceeds 2,627.

regulatoryResponse.regulator: Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC)
regulatoryResponse.actionType: Public warning and referral to police — suspected unlicensed VASP activity and market misconduct
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Warning issued. Matter referred to Hong Kong Police. SFC also reminded licensed VATPs of obligations regarding third-party promoters. Criminal proceedings ongoing.

regulatoryResponse.regulator: Hong Kong Police Force (HKPF) — Commercial Crime Bureau
regulatoryResponse.actionType: Criminal investigation — fraud, money laundering, unlicensed dealing in securities
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Over 60 individuals arrested. Criminal proceedings against defendants ongoing as of review date.

rarm.explanation: Legal & Jurisdictional: JPEX's primary failure was operating without a VATP licence, representing a fundamental legal compliance failure under HK law. The case illustrates that platforms serving HK residents are subject to SFC jurisdiction regardless of the location of their corporate entity. Custody & Asset Control: investors lost access to funds when JPEX raised withdrawal fees to punitive levels, suggesting either inadequate asset segregation or deliberate obstruction — the exact mechanics are subject to pending criminal proceedings. KYC/AML: the involvement of multiple KOL promoters and the scale of retail investor participation suggests inadequate investor protection and AML screening procedures.

lessons: The JPEX case illustrates the risk posed to retail investors by unregulated virtual asset platforms that aggressively market high-yield products without regulatory oversight. For Hong Kong investors in tokenized assets, the case underlines the importance of verifying VATP licensing status through the SFC's publicly maintained list of licenced VATPs before depositing funds. The SFC's licensing regime, which requires platforms to segregate client assets, maintain minimum capital, and implement investor protection measures, is designed specifically to prevent these failures.

title: USDC Temporary De-peg Following Silicon Valley Bank Failure
hkRelevance: Circle has applied for authorisation under Hong Kong's Stablecoins Ordinance. The SVB incident is documented as a precedent case illustrating reserve concentration risk under HKMA's evaluation framework.
estimatedLossNote: No permanent losses to USDC holders. Circle's USD 3.3 billion in USDC reserves held at SVB were subsequently guaranteed by the US Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC), and USDC restored its USD 1.00 peg by 13 March 2023.
summary: On 10 March 2023, Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) was placed into FDIC receivership following a bank run. Circle Internet Financial disclosed that approximately USD 3.3 billion of its approximately USD 40 billion USDC reserve funds were held in cash accounts at SVB, representing approximately 8% of total reserves. USDC traded below USD 1.00 on secondary markets from 10-13 March 2023, reaching approximately USD 0.87 at its lowest. The US government's 12 March 2023 announcement that all SVB depositors would be made whole caused USDC to restore its peg. No USDC holder suffered a permanent loss.
narrative:
USD Coin (USDC) is a regulated stablecoin issued by Circle Internet Financial. Circle publishes monthly reserve attestations prepared by Deloitte & Touche LLP. At the time of the SVB failure, Circle held approximately USD 40 billion in USDC reserves, the majority in US Treasury securities and money market funds. Approximately USD 3.3 billion was held in cash at SVB, representing roughly 8% of total reserves.

Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) was closed by California state regulators on 10 March 2023 and placed into FDIC receivership. Initial FDIC announcements indicated that uninsured deposits — those exceeding the standard USD 250,000 guarantee — might not be fully recovered. Circle disclosed the USD 3.3 billion SVB exposure via social media and its website on 10 March 2023.

Market participants, uncertain about recovery of the SVB-held reserves, began selling USDC on secondary markets. USDC's spot price on major exchanges fell to approximately USD 0.87 on 11 March 2023. Because Circle's reserve had over 90% in high-quality liquid assets unaffected by the SVB failure, the de-peg was driven by market uncertainty rather than actual reserve impairment.

On 12 March 2023, the US Treasury, Federal Reserve, and FDIC announced that all SVB depositors, including uninsured depositors, would have full access to their funds. Following this announcement, USDC began recovering and restored its USD 1.00 peg by the morning of 13 March 2023. Circle confirmed that all SVB-held reserves were accessible.

The incident highlighted the reserve concentration risk inherent in holding stablecoin cash reserves at a single financial institution. It prompted discussions among stablecoin issuers and regulators about reserve diversification requirements. The HKMA's stablecoin consultation framework explicitly addressed concentration risk in reserve custody arrangements, partly as a lesson derived from this incident.

timeline.event: Silvergate Capital announces voluntary liquidation. Silicon Valley Bank discloses USD 1.8 billion loss on securities sales.
California DFPI closes SVB and appoints FDIC as receiver. Circle discloses USD 3.3B in USDC reserves held at SVB.
USDC spot price falls to approximately USD 0.87 on secondary markets.
US Treasury, Federal Reserve, and FDIC announce all SVB depositors will be made whole. USDC begins recovering.
USDC restores USD 1.00 peg. Circle confirms SVB reserves are accessible and fully recovered.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Receivership — SVB taken into FDIC receivership; all depositors made whole under systemic risk exception
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Full depositor recovery announced. No stablecoin-specific enforcement action.

sarm.explanation: Reserve quality: the incident demonstrated that reserve concentration at a single financial institution creates a correlation risk not visible in reserve attestations that report only aggregate figures. USDC's reserves were high-quality in composition (>90% Treasuries and MMFs) but the 8% SVB cash allocation created a single-point-of-failure that, while ultimately benign due to FDIC intervention, caused significant secondary market disruption. Redemption and governance were not implicated: Circle's redemption mechanism functioned normally throughout, and there is no evidence of governance failure.

lessons: The USDC SVB de-peg illustrates that reserve quality assessments must account for custodian concentration risk, not only asset class quality. Even a stablecoin with a conservative reserve portfolio can experience secondary market de-pegging if a meaningful fraction of reserves is held at a single institution under stress. Issuers and regulators have subsequently emphasised reserve diversification across custodians and jurisdictions. The SARM framework's reserve quality dimension should evaluate both asset quality and custodian diversification when public information permits.

title: Silvergate Bank Voluntary Wind-Down and Crypto Settlement Network Closure
hkRelevance: Silvergate Bank operated the Silvergate Exchange Network (SEN), which provided 24/7 real-time US dollar settlement infrastructure for crypto-to-fiat transactions between institutional market participants globally, including entities with Hong Kong operations. SEN's abrupt closure on 8 March 2023 disrupted institutional dollar settlement for crypto exchanges and OTC desks including those serving Asian markets. The failure occurred alongside Signature Bank and Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023, collectively representing the most significant disruption to crypto market banking infrastructure since the industry's early years.
estimatedLossNote: Silvergate Bank voluntarily liquidated and confirmed that all depositors would be fully repaid from the bank's own assets, which exceeded its liabilities at the point of wind-down. No depositor losses were confirmed. The bank reported a loss of approximately USD 1 billion on the sale of held-to-maturity debt securities liquidated to meet customer withdrawals in Q4 2022. Financial losses to market participants from SEN closure are not publicly quantified.
summary: Silvergate Bank was a California-chartered bank that became a primary banking partner for cryptocurrency exchanges and institutional crypto market participants, operating the Silvergate Exchange Network (SEN) — a proprietary real-time US dollar payment network running 24/7. Following the FTX collapse in November 2022, Silvergate experienced significant customer withdrawals and reported a USD 1 billion loss on forced sale of held-to-maturity securities. On 8 March 2023, Silvergate Capital Corporation announced the voluntary wind-down of Silvergate Bank, immediately shutting down the SEN. This closure disrupted real-time US dollar settlement infrastructure for crypto institutional participants days before the Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank failures in the same week.
narrative:
Silvergate Bank, a California state-chartered bank and subsidiary of Silvergate Capital Corporation (NYSE: SI), pivoted toward serving the cryptocurrency industry beginning around 2013. By 2022, it had become one of the primary banking infrastructure providers for institutional cryptocurrency market participants in the United States, operating accounts for numerous major exchanges, OTC desks, stablecoin issuers, and crypto-focused investment firms.

Silvergate's flagship product was the Silvergate Exchange Network (SEN), a proprietary real-time payment network that allowed institutional clients to send and receive US dollar wire transfers 24/7/365 — a capability absent from conventional banking systems that operate only during weekday banking hours. The SEN was extensively used by crypto exchanges and market makers to settle large-volume fiat transactions at any time of day or night.

The collapse of FTX and Alameda Research in November 2022 created significant uncertainty about Silvergate's exposure to the FTX estate, as Silvergate had provided banking services to both. In Q4 2022, Silvergate was forced to sell approximately USD 5.2 billion in debt securities from its held-to-maturity portfolio to meet customer withdrawal requests, realising a loss of approximately USD 1 billion. The US Department of Justice was also reportedly investigating Silvergate's transaction monitoring and anti-money laundering practices with respect to FTX and related entities.

On 8 March 2023, Silvergate Capital Corporation announced the voluntary wind-down of Silvergate Bank, with the SEN closed immediately. Silvergate stated that the bank's assets exceeded its liabilities, that all deposits would be repaid in full, and that it was conducting an orderly wind-down under California state regulatory supervision. The loss of SEN created immediate disruption to institutional crypto dollar settlement infrastructure — days before the FDIC seizure of Silicon Valley Bank (10 March 2023) and Signature Bank (12 March 2023).

timeline.event: FTX files for bankruptcy. Silvergate's exposure to FTX and Alameda Research creates market uncertainty; customers begin withdrawing deposits.
Silvergate discloses Q4 2022 losses of approximately USD 1 billion from forced sale of held-to-maturity securities to meet customer withdrawals.
Silvergate Capital Corporation announces voluntary wind-down of Silvergate Bank. SEN closed immediately. Bank states all deposits will be repaid from existing assets.
California DFPI seizes Silicon Valley Bank and places it in FDIC receivership.
NYDFS seizes Signature Bank. US authorities announce all depositors at SVB and Signature Bank will be made whole.

regulatoryResponse.regulator: California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI)
regulatoryResponse.actionType: Oversight of voluntary wind-down
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Voluntary wind-down approved; depositors received full repayment. No FDIC receivership required.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Criminal investigation — BSA violations, AML practices relating to FTX and related entities
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Investigation reported in contemporaneous media. No confirmed charges against Silvergate Bank entity published as of review date.

sarm.explanation: Governance: Silvergate's failure to adequately monitor and report suspicious transactions through its banking platform — the subject of the DOJ investigation and congressional inquiries — illustrates how governance deficiencies in financial intermediaries serving the crypto sector can create systemic risk. The SEN's role in facilitating FTX and Alameda transactions raises questions about the due diligence and monitoring obligations of banks providing infrastructure to virtual asset platforms.

rarm.explanation: Legal & Jurisdictional: the DOJ investigation illustrates that banks providing infrastructure to virtual asset platforms are subject to BSA and AML obligations in respect of their customers' activities. KYC/AML & Permissioning: the investigation centred on whether Silvergate adequately monitored SEN transactions for suspicious patterns related to fraud. Settlement Finality: SEN's closure immediately disrupted real-time USD settlement finality for institutional crypto market participants — illustrating the systemic importance of banking infrastructure to the settlement layer. Secondary Market Liquidity: Silvergate's collapse, alongside SVB and Signature, materially reduced available banking infrastructure for US crypto market participants in March 2023.

lessons: Silvergate's voluntary wind-down — alongside the simultaneous FDIC seizures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank — illustrates the concentration risk created when a significant portion of an industry's banking relationships is concentrated in a small number of specialist financial institutions. The simultaneous loss of three crypto-friendly banks in one week in March 2023 demonstrated that banking infrastructure for crypto markets is a systemic risk factor. For tokenized asset issuers, the case highlights the importance of bank relationship diversification and the development of alternative settlement mechanisms. It also illustrates the regulatory scrutiny that banks providing services to virtual asset platforms face, particularly regarding AML transaction monitoring obligations.

title: Gemini Earn Programme Freeze and Multi-Year Recovery (2022–2024)
issuerOrOperator: Gemini Trust Company, LLC / Genesis Global Capital, LLC
hkRelevance: The enforced transparency about Gemini Earn's counterparty exposure to Genesis — itself exposed to the Three Arrows Capital and FTX collapses — is a reference case for how yield product counterparty chains can transmit losses to retail investors. The NY Attorney General's findings about undisclosed counterparty risk have informed the HKMA's approach to requiring transparency in virtual asset yield products offered in or marketed to Hong Kong investors.
estimatedLossNote: No permanent losses to Gemini Earn users were ultimately recorded. Approximately USD 900 million to USD 1.1 billion in Earn user assets were frozen from November 2022 through recovery in 2024. Following the global settlement, Earn users received full in-kind recovery of their deposited assets — representing approximately 232% of the USD value at the time Genesis froze withdrawals, reflecting the price appreciation of the underlying cryptocurrencies during the period. Secondary market participants who sold Earn-related claims at distressed prices would have incurred losses not captured in this figure.
summary: Gemini Earn was a yield product offered by Gemini Trust Company through which retail users lent cryptocurrency assets to Genesis Global Capital in exchange for interest payments. On 16 November 2022, Genesis halted all withdrawals and new loan originations following losses from the Three Arrows Capital collapse and FTX-related counterparty exposures, freezing approximately USD 900 million to USD 1.1 billion in Gemini Earn user funds. Genesis filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on 19 January 2023. After a protracted multi-regulator enforcement process, Gemini settled with the New York Attorney General for USD 50 million, and a global settlement returned the full in-kind value of Earn assets to users — approximately USD 2.18 billion — by May 2024.
narrative:
Gemini Earn was launched in 2021 as a yield product within the Gemini cryptocurrency exchange platform. Earn users lent their cryptocurrency assets to Genesis Global Capital under a loan agreement; Genesis deployed these assets in institutional lending and proprietary trading. The arrangement was structured so that Earn users were creditors of Genesis rather than beneficiaries of a regulated deposit scheme, a structure the New York Attorney General subsequently characterised as a material omission in Gemini's investor disclosures.

Genesis Global Capital incurred significant losses in 2022: first, from exposure to Three Arrows Capital (3AC), a crypto hedge fund that defaulted in June 2022 with Genesis reportedly owed approximately USD 2.36 billion; second, from exposure to FTX and Alameda Research, which filed for bankruptcy in November 2022. On 16 November 2022, Genesis halted all new loan originations and redemptions from existing loans, effectively freezing Gemini Earn user assets.

Gemini co-founder Cameron Winklevoss publicly demanded that Digital Currency Group (Genesis's parent) provide liquidity to resolve the Earn freeze, publishing an open letter in January 2023 accusing DCG CEO Barry Silbert of bad faith. Genesis filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on 19 January 2023 with approximately USD 5.1 billion in creditor claims. The New York Attorney General filed suit against Gemini, Genesis, and Digital Currency Group in October 2023, alleging that Gemini had misled Earn users about the risks and structure of the programme.

Gemini settled with the New York Attorney General on 29 May 2024, agreeing to pay USD 50 million in restitution to former Earn users. As part of a global settlement involving the Genesis bankruptcy estate, Gemini Earn users received full in-kind recovery of their deposited assets, representing approximately USD 2.18 billion — approximately 232% of the USD value of the assets at the time of the freeze due to appreciation of the underlying cryptocurrencies. Cameron Winklevoss contributed USD 40 million personally to the Earn recovery fund.

Genesis separately settled SEC charges for USD 21 million in March 2024, admitting that the Gemini Earn product constituted an unregistered securities offering. Gemini settled separate CFTC charges for USD 5 million in January 2025 related to misleading statements about Bitcoin futures markets — a distinct matter from the Earn programme.

timeline.event: Genesis Global Capital halts all withdrawals and new loan originations. Gemini Earn user funds frozen.
Genesis Global Capital files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the US Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of New York.
New York Attorney General files suit against Gemini, Genesis, and Digital Currency Group, alleging fraud and misrepresentation to Earn investors.
Genesis agrees to pay USD 21 million to settle SEC charges that Gemini Earn constituted an unregistered securities offering.
Gemini settles with New York AG for USD 50 million. Global settlement returns approximately USD 2.18 billion in-kind to Earn users — full recovery of deposited assets plus accrued appreciation.
Gemini agrees to pay USD 5 million to settle CFTC charges relating to misleading statements about Bitcoin futures markets (a separate matter from Earn).

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Civil enforcement — investor fraud, unregistered securities, material misrepresentation
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Settled May 2024. Gemini pays USD 50 million; full in-kind recovery of approximately USD 2.18 billion returned to Earn users.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Civil enforcement — unregistered securities offering (Genesis)
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Genesis agreed to pay USD 21 million civil penalty to settle charges that Gemini Earn was an unregistered securities offering.

regulatoryResponse.actionType: Civil enforcement — misleading statements about Bitcoin futures manipulation resistance (separate from Earn)
regulatoryResponse.outcome: Gemini agreed to pay USD 5 million settlement. Separate from Earn programme.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: Gemini Earn users were effectively unsecured creditors of Genesis, not holders of a segregated or protected deposit. The absence of asset segregation meant that Genesis's losses from 3AC and FTX directly impaired Earn users. Settlement Finality: the sixteen-month period during which Earn assets were frozen illustrates the settlement finality risk of yield structures where underlying assets are deployed in counterparty lending. Legal & Jurisdictional: the NY AG's finding that Gemini misled Earn users about the counterparty structure and risk establishes that yield product providers bear disclosure obligations to retail participants comparable to those of registered investment products.

lessons: The Gemini Earn case demonstrates that retail yield products relying on opaque counterparty lending chains create undisclosed risks that may not be apparent to depositors. Earn users were effectively unsecured creditors of Genesis Global Capital, but were presented with a simple interest-rate product without adequate disclosure of the counterparty's financial condition. The case also illustrates how contagion can propagate through interconnected crypto lending platforms: Genesis's losses from Three Arrows Capital and FTX were the direct cause of the Earn freeze. The full in-kind recovery outcome — though ultimately positive for users — was not a predictable result of the legal structure, and relied on price appreciation during the freeze period rather than platform solvency.

title: BlockFi Bankruptcy Following FTX Contagion
hkRelevance: BlockFi's collapse as a direct consequence of its concentrated exposure to FTX is a reference case for counterparty concentration risk in crypto yield and lending platforms. The US state and SEC enforcement actions against BlockFi's interest accounts in February 2022 — months before its bankruptcy — illustrate that yield-bearing crypto accounts attract securities regulation, precedents relevant to the HKMA's and SFC's regulatory framework for virtual asset yield products offered to Hong Kong investors.
estimatedLossNote: BlockFi held approximately USD 1.2 billion in assets with FTX and its affiliated trading firm Alameda Research at the time of FTX's bankruptcy filing. Total client funds on the BlockFi platform at the time of its own bankruptcy filing are not confirmed in public records reviewed at the time of writing. Creditor recovery rates through the bankruptcy liquidation process varied by account type and are subject to ongoing proceedings.
summary: BlockFi Inc. was a US-based cryptocurrency lending and interest account platform that received a USD 400 million credit facility from FTX in July 2022 following losses from the Three Arrows Capital collapse. When FTX filed for bankruptcy on 11 November 2022, BlockFi immediately suspended withdrawals and client transactions due to its approximately USD 1.2 billion exposure to the FTX estate. BlockFi filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on 28 November 2022. In February 2022, BlockFi had already settled with the SEC and multiple US state regulators for USD 100 million for operating unregistered securities through its BlockFi Interest Accounts product — a settlement that presaged the company's ultimate insolvency.
narrative:
BlockFi was founded in 2017 and became one of the largest US-based crypto lending platforms, offering retail users interest-bearing accounts (BlockFi Interest Accounts) backed by institutional lending and structured products. At its peak in 2021, BlockFi reported approximately USD 15 billion in assets under management.

In February 2022, BlockFi reached a USD 100 million settlement with the SEC and a consortium of US state securities regulators over its BlockFi Interest Account product, which regulators determined constituted an unregistered offer and sale of securities. BlockFi agreed to stop offering the product to US customers, to register a new compliant product, and to pay USD 50 million to the SEC and USD 50 million to state regulators. This was among the first enforcement actions against a US crypto yield platform.

In June 2022, the Three Arrows Capital hedge fund collapsed. BlockFi had significant exposure to 3AC through collateralised loans, resulting in reported losses of approximately USD 80 million. In July 2022, FTX provided BlockFi with a USD 400 million revolving credit facility and an option to acquire BlockFi, effectively positioning FTX as a backstop for BlockFi's liquidity.

When FTX filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on 11 November 2022, BlockFi immediately suspended client withdrawals and transactions. BlockFi disclosed that it had approximately USD 1.2 billion in assets held at or subject to claims against the FTX estate. BlockFi filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy itself on 28 November 2022 in the US Bankruptcy Court for the District of New Jersey with liabilities to more than 100,000 creditors.

The BlockFi bankruptcy estate pursued claims against the FTX estate and Alameda Research. Creditor distributions were subject to the resolution of these cross-claims, with the outcome partly dependent on the FTX bankruptcy proceedings. The liquidation process extended through 2024–2025.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: BlockFi's concentrated exposure to FTX — approximately USD 1.2 billion in assets held with or claims against the FTX estate — illustrates custody and counterparty concentration risk in centralised crypto lending platforms that deploy customer assets through single counterparties. Settlement Finality: client withdrawal suspension immediately upon FTX's bankruptcy demonstrates how rapidly settlement finality can be compromised when platform liquidity depends on a single stressed counterparty. Legal & Jurisdictional: BlockFi's February 2022 securities enforcement settlement established that interest-bearing crypto accounts constitute regulated securities in the US — a precedent with direct implications for similar products in other jurisdictions including Hong Kong.

lessons: The BlockFi collapse illustrates two compounding risks for crypto lending platforms: the legal risk of operating unregistered yield products without regulatory approval (the February 2022 securities settlement); and the catastrophic counterparty concentration risk of relying on a single institutional partner for emergency liquidity. The sequence — securities violation, FTX credit support, FTX bankruptcy, BlockFi insolvency — demonstrates how regulatory non-compliance can create dependencies on non-compliant counterparties, with cascading consequences for end users. The case reinforces the need for mandatory counterparty diversification and asset segregation requirements for any platform offering yield products backed by external lending.

title: FTX Exchange Collapse and Bankruptcy
issuerOrOperator: FTX Trading Ltd. / West Realm Shires Inc.
hkRelevance: FTX had a significant retail user base in Hong Kong. The SFC cited the FTX collapse as one of the precipitating factors behind the urgency of implementing Hong Kong's Virtual Asset Service Provider licensing regime. The SFC and Hong Kong Police received numerous complaints from HK retail investors.
estimatedLossNote: USD 8.7 billion customer fund shortfall per FTX bankruptcy proceedings. This represents the gap between customer claims and available assets. Individual creditor recovery has been subject to ongoing bankruptcy proceedings; the FTX bankruptcy estate announced creditor distribution plans beginning 2024.
summary: FTX was one of the world's largest cryptocurrency exchanges until November 2022, when a CoinDesk report revealed that a substantial portion of Alameda Research's reported assets were FTT tokens issued by FTX itself. A subsequent announcement by Binance CEO Changpeng Zhao of planned FTT liquidation triggered a bank run. FTX was unable to process withdrawal requests; the exchange filed for bankruptcy on 11 November 2022. Founder Samuel Bankman-Fried was charged with wire fraud, securities fraud, and money laundering, and was convicted in November 2023. The bankruptcy estate estimated a customer shortfall of approximately USD 8.7 billion.
narrative:
FTX was incorporated in Antigua and Barbuda and operated internationally, including significant operations in The Bahamas (FTX International) and the United States (FTX.US). The exchange was founded by Samuel Bankman-Fried and Gary Wang in 2019 and reached peak reported valuations of approximately USD 32 billion.

On 2 November 2022, CoinDesk published a report revealing that the balance sheet of Alameda Research, the trading firm closely affiliated with FTX, consisted primarily of FTT tokens — a cryptocurrency issued by FTX itself. The report raised concerns about the circular relationship between FTX and Alameda and the quality of Alameda's balance sheet assets. On 6 November 2022, Binance CEO Changpeng Zhao announced plans to liquidate Binance's FTT holdings citing risk management concerns. This triggered significant FTT selling and FTX withdrawal requests.

Within days, FTX was unable to process customer withdrawal requests. On 11 November 2022, FTX filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in Delaware, and Bankman-Fried resigned as CEO. Subsequent bankruptcy filings revealed that customer deposits had been transferred to Alameda Research, which had used them to fund investments and operating expenses. The bankruptcy estate identified a customer fund shortfall of approximately USD 8.7 billion.

Samuel Bankman-Fried was arrested in The Bahamas on 12 December 2022 and extradited to the United States. He was convicted by a US federal jury in November 2023 on seven counts of wire fraud and conspiracy. He was sentenced to 25 years in federal prison in March 2024. FTX co-founder Gary Wang and former CEO Caroline Ellison cooperated with prosecutors and received reduced sentences.

The FTX collapse accelerated regulatory responses in multiple jurisdictions. The SFC cited FTX as a catalyst for fast-tracking mandatory VATP licensing requirements in Hong Kong. The EU accelerated implementation of MiCA custody and asset segregation provisions. In the US, the collapse reinvigorated Congressional debates on cryptocurrency exchange regulation.

Bankman-Fried sentenced to 25 years in federal prison.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: the core FTX failure was the commingling of customer assets with proprietary trading funds (Alameda Research), which is a fundamental custody control failure. Any tokenized asset platform that does not maintain strict asset segregation is exposed to analogous risk. KYC/AML & Permissioning: FTX lacked adequate internal controls, compliance functions, and board oversight. Secondary Market Liquidity: FTX's insolvency caused significant secondary market disruption and FTT token illiquidity, illustrating how platform-specific token holdings can amplify exchange insolvency. Legal & Jurisdictional: FTX's multi-jurisdictional incorporation structure (Antigua, Bahamas, US) created regulatory arbitrage and complicated the bankruptcy proceedings.

lessons: The FTX collapse illustrates that the absence of asset segregation, independent governance, and functional compliance infrastructure creates conditions for catastrophic customer fund loss even at large, well-resourced platforms. For tokenized asset platforms, the RARM custody layer remains critical: customer assets must be ring-fenced from operator balance sheets and subject to independent verification. The case also demonstrates that exchange-issued tokens (FTT) as collateral or reserve assets create dangerous circular dependencies.

title: Wintermute Market Maker — Profanity Address Vulnerability and USD 162M Loss
hkRelevance: Wintermute is a London-based algorithmic market maker with significant presence in Asian crypto markets. The incident illustrates the private key management risks inherent in DeFi market-making operations and the vulnerability created by the use of vanity address generators with insufficient entropy. Institutional participants in tokenized asset markets relying on market makers should consider counterparty operational security risk when evaluating liquidity provider resilience.
estimatedLossNote: USD 162.2 million in various cryptocurrency tokens drained from Wintermute's DeFi operations wallet. No user or counterparty funds were lost; the loss was entirely from Wintermute's own proprietary trading portfolio. Wintermute remained solvent and continued operations. The attacker was offered a 10% white-hat bounty but funds were not returned.
summary: On 20 September 2022, Wintermute Trading Ltd., a leading algorithmic market maker, suffered a hack of its DeFi operations wallet resulting in a loss of approximately USD 162.2 million in cryptocurrency tokens. The attack exploited a vulnerability in the Profanity vanity address generator — a tool used to create visually distinctive Ethereum addresses — which generated private keys with insufficient randomness, making them susceptible to brute-force derivation. CEO Evgeny Gaevoy publicly acknowledged the hack on the same day. No counterparty or client funds were affected, and Wintermute remained solvent. The attacker did not respond to a 10% white-hat bounty offer and funds were not recovered.
narrative:
Wintermute Trading is a London-based algorithmic market maker that provides liquidity across centralised and decentralised cryptocurrency exchanges. It is a significant participant in DeFi protocols and holds one of the largest DeFi market-making portfolios among institutional players.

In September 2022, a security researcher independently identified a critical vulnerability in the Profanity vanity Ethereum address generator: the tool used a 32-bit random seed to generate private keys, significantly below the 256-bit security standard. This weakness made it computationally feasible to brute-force private keys for any address generated by Profanity. On 15 September 2022, 1inch Network published a public warning about the vulnerability.

On 20 September 2022, an attacker exploited this vulnerability to derive the private key for Wintermute's DeFi operations address — an address that had been generated using the Profanity tool. With the private key in hand, the attacker drained approximately USD 162.2 million in tokens.

Wintermute CEO Evgeny Gaevoy publicly acknowledged the hack via social media, confirming that only Wintermute's own DeFi portfolio had been affected and that CeFi operations, OTC trading, and counterparty obligations remained unaffected. Wintermute was solvent and would continue to honour all counterparty obligations. The company offered the attacker a 10% white-hat bounty (approximately USD 16.2 million) to return the remaining funds; the attacker did not respond and funds were not recovered.

The incident prompted widespread review across the industry of Ethereum addresses generated using Profanity and similar vanity address tools with weak randomness. Multiple other protocols and individuals discovered their own addresses were potentially vulnerable. The technical flaw in Profanity had been independently identified and disclosed by 1inch five days before the Wintermute attack.

timeline.event: 1inch Network publishes a warning about the Profanity vanity address generator vulnerability, noting that all Profanity-generated addresses may be susceptible to private key compromise.
Attacker exploits Profanity vulnerability to derive Wintermute DeFi operations wallet private key. Approximately USD 162.2 million drained in multiple on-chain transactions.
Wintermute CEO Evgeny Gaevoy publicly confirms hack, confirms no counterparty or client funds affected, and offers 10% white-hat bounty to attacker.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: the core vulnerability was inadequate private key generation security — a fundamental custody layer failure. The use of a vanity address generator with insufficient entropy (32-bit seed) to secure a USD 162 million institutional operations wallet illustrates that custody security for large DeFi portfolios requires the same rigour as traditional institutional key management. Secondary Market Liquidity: Wintermute is a major liquidity provider across DeFi and CeFi markets; the USD 162 million loss, while not causing counterparty failures, temporarily reduced its DeFi liquidity provision capacity.

lessons: The Wintermute hack demonstrates that institutional private key management in DeFi environments requires the same rigour as traditional financial key management infrastructure. The specific vulnerability — vanity address generators with insufficient cryptographic entropy — illustrates that convenience features (memorable wallet addresses) can introduce catastrophic security weaknesses. Institutional participants in DeFi markets should audit all wallet addresses for potential exposure to weak key generation tools and implement hardware security module (HSM) based key management for all operationally significant addresses. The 1inch warning about the Profanity vulnerability was published five days before the Wintermute attack, highlighting the importance of timely security disclosure monitoring.

title: Tornado Cash OFAC Designation and Developer Prosecutions
estimatedLossNote: No direct financial losses to users from the OFAC designation itself. Circle froze approximately USD 75,000 in USDC held in Tornado Cash smart contract addresses following the designation. Broader economic impact on users unable to access mixed funds is not quantified in public records.
summary: On 8 August 2022, the US Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) added Tornado Cash and 44 associated Ethereum smart contract addresses to the Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) list, making it a civil and criminal offence for US persons to interact with the protocol. Tornado Cash was a privacy mixer protocol through which approximately USD 7 billion had passed since 2019, including funds linked to the Lazarus Group (North Korea). The designation set a precedent for sanctioning immutable open-source smart contract code directly. Developer Roman Storm was arrested in August 2023 and convicted in November 2024. Co-developer Roman Semenov was simultaneously indicted.
narrative:
Tornado Cash was an Ethereum-based cryptocurrency mixing protocol that allowed users to obscure the on-chain transaction history of Ether and ERC-20 tokens, including stablecoins. The protocol operated through a series of smart contracts that accepted deposits and issued cryptographic proofs enabling withdrawals to new addresses with no on-chain link to the original deposit address.

OFAC estimated that Tornado Cash had been used to launder more than USD 7 billion since 2019. Among the most prominent uses cited were the Lazarus Group (a North Korean state-sponsored threat actor) moving approximately USD 455 million stolen in the Ronin Bridge exploit, and additional proceeds from the Harmony Horizon Bridge hack.

On 8 August 2022, OFAC designated Tornado Cash and 44 specific Ethereum smart contract addresses under Executive Order 13694. This was a novel application of sanctions: rather than targeting an entity or individual, OFAC sanctioned the code itself. Circle Internet Financial blocked USDC transfers to and from the designated Tornado Cash contract addresses within hours of the designation.

In August 2023, Dutch authorities, working with the US, arrested Roman Storm, a US-based developer. Storm was indicted on charges of conspiracy to commit money laundering, sanctions violations, and operating an unlicensed money transmitting business. He was convicted by a federal jury in November 2024. Co-developer Roman Semenov, based in Russia, was simultaneously placed on the SDN list.

Several users who had received unsolicited Tornado Cash transactions ("dusting attacks") challenged the OFAC designation in federal court. A Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals ruling in November 2024 held that OFAC had exceeded its authority by sanctioning the immutable smart contract code, though the designation of the associated persons and organisation remains in force. The case remains significant for the question of whether open-source protocol code can itself be subject to sanctions.
