Roman Storm arrested in US on money laundering and sanctions charges. Roman Semenov added to OFAC SDN list.
Dutch court convicts Alexey Pertsev of money laundering; sentenced to 5 years 4 months.
Roman Storm convicted by US federal jury on all three counts.
US Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals rules OFAC exceeded authority in sanctioning immutable smart contract code; remands to lower court. Sanctions against persons and organisation remain.

rarm.explanation: KYC/AML & Permissioning layer: the Tornado Cash case established that tokenization and DeFi infrastructure is subject to AML controls regardless of the absence of a centralised operator, and that stablecoin issuers (including Circle) must comply with OFAC designations affecting smart contract addresses. This has direct implications for any tokenized asset that relies on privacy-enhancing infrastructure. Legal & Jurisdictional layer: the case demonstrates significant cross-border jurisdictional uncertainty for open-source protocol developers, and that sanctions regimes can apply to protocol-layer infrastructure, not just custodians and issuers.

lessons: The Tornado Cash case illustrates that open-source permissionless infrastructure is not exempt from sanctions regimes or AML obligations when it demonstrably facilitates money laundering at scale. For tokenized asset infrastructure, the case implies that any protocol that lacks permissioning controls may be subject to regulatory designation, regardless of the decentralised nature of its operation. The partial reversal by the Fifth Circuit in respect of the immutable contract code adds significant legal uncertainty about the boundaries of sanctions authority over on-chain infrastructure.

title: Celsius Network Withdrawal Freeze and Bankruptcy
hkRelevance: The Celsius bankruptcy highlighted risks in centralised crypto yield platforms operating without banking regulation, a concern shared by the HKMA and SFC in Hong Kong. The case informed regulatory discussions about the need to regulate crypto lending and yield products and contributed to the SFC's heightened scrutiny of virtual asset platforms offering high-yield deposit products to retail investors.
estimatedLossNote: Approximate total unsecured creditor claims filed in the US Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of New York. Actual distributions to creditors beginning in early 2024 represented approximately USD 3 billion in recovery value — approximately 67 cents per dollar of claim. Individual recovery rates varied by account type. The USD 4.7 billion figure is approximate and should be verified against final court-confirmed distribution records.
summary: Celsius Network was a centralised cryptocurrency yield platform founded in 2017 that offered retail and institutional users interest-bearing accounts backed by crypto lending. On 12 June 2022, Celsius abruptly suspended all withdrawals, swaps, and transfers, freezing approximately USD 4.7 billion in user assets. Celsius filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on 13 July 2022. Subsequent proceedings revealed pervasive commingling of customer and company assets, undisclosed losses, and artificial inflation of the CEL token price. CEO Alex Mashinsky was arrested in July 2023, indicted on fraud charges, and pleaded guilty in December 2024. Celsius emerged from bankruptcy on 31 January 2024 with creditors receiving approximately 67 cents per dollar of claims.
narrative:
Celsius Network operated a yield-generating product called Celsius Earn, through which retail and institutional users deposited cryptocurrency assets in exchange for weekly interest payments. Celsius deployed these user deposits in DeFi lending protocols, collateralised lending positions, and proprietary trading to generate the returns distributed to users. At its peak in 2021, Celsius held approximately USD 20 billion in assets under management and reported over 1.7 million registered users globally.

On 12 June 2022, Celsius Network published a brief notice suspending all withdrawals, swaps, and transfers between accounts, citing extreme market conditions and the need to preserve liquidity. No financial details were disclosed at the time. The suspension trapped billions of dollars in user funds with no clear timeline for recovery, causing acute distress to retail depositors who had treated the platform as a savings account.

Celsius filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in the US Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of New York on 13 July 2022. Subsequent filings and court-appointed examiner reports revealed significant mismanagement: customer Earn deposits had been commingled with company funds and used to support Celsius's own trading positions, pay operational expenses, and fund the artificial buy-back of CEL tokens to inflate the token price.

The US Department of Justice, Securities and Exchange Commission, and Commodity Futures Trading Commission filed parallel charges against CEO Alex Mashinsky and then-Chief Revenue Officer Roni Cohen-Pavon in July 2023. The DOJ alleged that Mashinsky had misrepresented the safety of Celsius's business model while secretly selling CEL tokens at prices he knew to be inflated by company buy-backs. Cohen-Pavon cooperated with prosecutors and received a reduced sentence. Mashinsky pleaded guilty in December 2024 to commodities fraud and market manipulation; sentencing was pending as of the review date.

The Celsius reorganisation plan was confirmed by the bankruptcy court in November 2023. Celsius emerged from bankruptcy on 31 January 2024, distributing remaining assets to creditors. Total creditor recovery was approximately USD 3 billion against approximately USD 4.7 billion in claims — a recovery rate of approximately 67 cents on the dollar. Creditors received a combination of cryptocurrency assets and equity interests in the successor entity.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: the core Celsius failure was the commingling of customer Earn deposits with company funds and proprietary trading positions — a fundamental custody segregation failure structurally identical to the FTX collapse. Settlement Finality: the indefinite withdrawal freeze meant users could not exit positions or realise the settlement finality of their deposit claims; the stETH illiquidity further prevented orderly resolution. Secondary Market Liquidity: the CEL token's artificial price inflation via undisclosed buy-backs created a misleading liquidity signal that masked the platform's actual financial position. Legal & Jurisdictional: the multi-agency prosecution of Mashinsky establishes precedents for criminal liability of yield-product operators who misrepresent the safety of their programmes to retail depositors.

lessons: The Celsius collapse illustrates that centralised crypto yield platforms face the same structural risks as unregulated deposit-taking institutions, but without capital adequacy requirements, asset segregation obligations, or deposit insurance. The commingling of customer Earn deposits with proprietary activities is structurally analogous to a bank misappropriating deposits for proprietary trading, and produces the same outcome when platform losses exceed the ability to meet withdrawal obligations. For tokenized asset platforms, the RARM custody layer is foundational: strict asset segregation between customer and operator assets, independently verified, is a non-negotiable requirement. The case also demonstrates that yield products offering above-market returns on crypto assets warrant the same regulatory scrutiny as traditional securities or deposit products.

title: TerraUSD / LUNA Ecosystem Collapse
estimatedLossNote: Approximate combined peak market capitalisation of LUNA (~USD 40B) and UST (~USD 18B) at the time of collapse. Individual holder losses are not separately quantified in public records. Final creditor recovery figures remain subject to ongoing bankruptcy proceedings.
summary: TerraUSD (UST) was an algorithmic stablecoin issued by Terraform Labs designed to maintain a 1:1 USD peg through a mint-and-burn mechanism with its sister token LUNA. In May 2022, large selling pressure against UST in the Curve Finance liquidity pool triggered a de-peg that the Luna Foundation Guard's USD 3.5 billion Bitcoin reserve fund could not arrest. Within days, the reflexive mint-and-burn mechanism produced hyperinflationary LUNA issuance, collapsing both tokens to near zero and resulting in estimated losses of approximately USD 40-60 billion in combined ecosystem market capitalisation. The incident is the largest algorithmic stablecoin failure on record and prompted regulatory responses in multiple jurisdictions.
narrative:
TerraUSD (UST) operated without exogenous hard-asset reserves. Instead, it relied on an algorithmic peg mechanism: one UST could always be minted by burning USD 1 worth of LUNA, and vice versa. The Luna Foundation Guard (LFG), established in January 2022, accumulated approximately USD 3.5 billion in Bitcoin to supplement this mechanism as an emergency reserve.

On 7 May 2022, approximately USD 350 million in UST was withdrawn from the Curve Finance 3pool, significantly disrupting the pool's balance. Additional large outflows followed. UST began trading below USD 0.99, triggering the algorithmic stabilisation mechanism, which caused large LUNA burns and issuance. The LFG deployed its Bitcoin reserves between 9 and 11 May 2022 in an attempt to defend the peg, but market selling pressure exceeded available reserves.

UST fell to approximately USD 0.30 on 11 May 2022 and continued declining. The stabilisation mechanism responded by issuing new LUNA at accelerating rates: LUNA's circulating supply expanded from approximately 346 million tokens to over 6.5 trillion within days, collapsing its price toward zero. On 13 May 2022, Terraform Labs halted the Terra blockchain to prevent further LUNA issuance. A subsequent hard fork produced a new chain (Terra 2.0) without UST, though recovery of losses to original holders was minimal.

The United States Securities and Exchange Commission filed charges against Terraform Labs co-founder Do Kwon and Terraform Labs in February 2023, alleging multiple counts of securities fraud and market manipulation. Do Kwon was arrested in Montenegro in March 2023 and extradited to the United States in December 2024. Terraform Labs reached a settlement with the SEC in June 2024, agreeing to pay approximately USD 4.47 billion in penalties, disgorgement, and interest.

The collapse demonstrated the structural fragility of algorithmic stablecoin designs that depend on reflexive token price mechanics for reserve management. The incident directly influenced regulatory responses in the European Union (MiCA Regulation, which explicitly restricts algorithmic stablecoins), the United States (proposed stablecoin legislation), and Hong Kong (the Stablecoins Ordinance, Cap. 649, which requires fully backed exogenous reserves).

Do Kwon arrested in Montenegro on fraudulent passport charges.
Terraform Labs and Do Kwon agree to pay USD 4.47 billion to settle SEC fraud charges.

sarm.explanation: Reserve quality: UST held no exogenous hard-asset reserves; the LFG Bitcoin reserve (~USD 3.5B) was wholly inadequate relative to the ~USD 18B UST circulating supply and was exhausted within days. Redemption mechanics: the algorithmic redemption mechanism (burn UST, mint LUNA) became self-defeating under conditions of simultaneous selling pressure on both tokens — the classic 'death spiral'. Governance: Terraform Labs executives made public statements about the security of UST reserves that the SEC subsequently characterised as fraudulent misrepresentations.

lessons: The TerraUSD collapse illustrates that an algorithmic stablecoin without exogenous high-quality liquid asset reserves cannot sustain a peg under reflexive market stress. The incident also demonstrates that reserve adequacy cannot be measured against current circulating supply alone — the reserve must be sufficient to absorb a run, not merely to support normal operations. Regulators in multiple jurisdictions subsequently adopted or proposed full-backing requirements that prohibit algorithmic reserve mechanisms. In the SARM framework, this incident maps to red signals across reserve quality, redemption mechanics, and governance — the three dimensions most consequential for stablecoin stability.

title: Ronin Bridge Smart Contract Exploit
estimatedLossNote: Approximately 173,600 ETH and 25.5 million USDC stolen on 23 March 2022. USD equivalent at time of exploit approximately USD 625 million. The US FBI attributed the theft to Lazarus Group (North Korean state-sponsored). Sky Mavis subsequently raised USD 150 million to compensate affected users.
summary: On 23 March 2022, the Ronin Network bridge — used to move assets between the Ethereum mainnet and the Axie Infinity sidechain — was exploited by attackers who compromised five of the nine validator private keys required to authorise withdrawals. The exploit, discovered on 29 March 2022, resulted in the theft of approximately 173,600 ETH and 25.5 million USDC, totalling approximately USD 625 million. The US FBI attributed the exploit to the Lazarus Group, a North Korean state-sponsored threat actor. Sky Mavis subsequently raised USD 150 million to reimburse affected users.
narrative:
The Ronin Network was a purpose-built Ethereum sidechain developed by Sky Mavis to support high-throughput transactions for its Axie Infinity gaming platform. A bridge contract allowed users to move Ether and ERC-20 tokens between Ethereum mainnet and the Ronin sidechain. Ronin used a proof-of-authority consensus mechanism where withdrawals required signatures from 5 of 9 designated validators.

On 23 March 2022, attackers compromised the private keys of five validators: four Sky Mavis nodes and one Axie DAO validator. The Axie DAO validator compromise was made possible by a legacy arrangement in which Sky Mavis had been granted signing authority to reduce transaction fees during a high-volume period, an arrangement that had not been revoked. With control of five validators, the attackers were able to authorise fraudulent withdrawal transactions.

The exploit was not discovered until 29 March 2022, when a user reported an inability to withdraw funds. Sky Mavis confirmed the exploit publicly on 29 March 2022. 173,600 ETH and 25.5 million USDC had been withdrawn in two transactions. The six-day detection gap is attributed to the absence of automated monitoring for abnormally large bridge withdrawals.

On 14 April 2022, the US Treasury OFAC sanctioned an Ethereum address associated with the hack, linking it to the Lazarus Group. The FBI formally attributed the exploit to Lazarus Group on 14 April 2022. The Lazarus Group had previously been linked to other large cryptocurrency thefts including the 2018 USD 571 million Coincheck hack.

Sky Mavis raised USD 150 million in a funding round led by Binance in April 2022 to reimburse affected users. The Ronin bridge was relaunched with a revised security architecture, including increased validator count and improved monitoring. USDC seized by US authorities linked to the exploit was partially returned to Sky Mavis.

rarm.explanation: Custody & Asset Control: the Ronin exploit resulted directly from inadequate key management practices — specifically, the retention of a legacy signing arrangement that expanded the effective attack surface. Bridge custody architecture requires rigorous key rotation, hardware security module (HSM) usage, and periodic access control reviews. Settlement Finality: the six-day detection gap allowed the stolen transactions to achieve finality on-chain. For tokenized assets, this illustrates that cross-chain bridges introduce settlement finality risk that is not present in single-chain deployments. KYC/AML: the Lazarus Group attribution and subsequent OFAC SDN designation required compliance responses from all US-connected exchanges and stablecoin issuers holding sanctioned addresses.

lessons: The Ronin exploit demonstrates that bridge infrastructure connecting separate blockchain networks represents a concentrated custody risk point. The governance failure — retaining a legacy signing authority beyond its intended period — illustrates that security architecture must be actively maintained, not merely designed correctly at inception. Detection latency (six days) substantially amplified the harm: real-time monitoring for abnormal bridge withdrawal volumes would have allowed earlier intervention. These lessons are directly applicable to cross-chain tokenized asset infrastructure.
